content.full_text:
File                                                September 26, 1979


                                                             m-79-091


Rich Eichman

Santa Monica Mountain Comprehensive Planning Commission


With regard to questions posed in Lee Rosenthal's memo dated
September 7, 1979, the following responses were given to
Tony Alperin:

The Santa Monica Mountain Comprehensive Planning Commission
(Commission) is a state agency. The Commission filed a
conflict of interest code with the Fair Political Practices
Commission in June 1978. The Commission (which has ceased
to exist) was authorized by statue of the State of California.
One member was a state officer, the Commission was financed
completely by the State and the jurisdiction included
Los Angeles and Ventura counties. (2 Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18249) The purpose of the Commission was to prepare
a plan for development of the area. The Commission only
had power to recommend. A final report by the Commission
was filed with the State Office of Planning and Research.

While the Commission is a state agency, the city of Los Angeles
is not required to disclose on it's lobbyist employer report,
the salaries paid to aides of a city councilman for the staff
work the aides do in connection with the councilman's
Commission duties. FPPC staff determined that the aides
are assisting the city councilman in carrying out his official
duties. Therefore, the city of Los Angeles would not be
required to report the aide's salaries on the lobbyist
employer report. (Government Code Section 86300(a))

In our phone conversation Tony Alperin stated that to the
best of his knowledge the city did not attempt to influence
the Commission in any way. The report issued by the Commission
was simply reviewed by the city and they took no action with
respect to the report. Consequently, Mr. Alperin has withdrawn
the question raised with respect to the city attempting to
influence the Commission.

State of California

Memorandum

To  :  Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date  :  September 7, 197

From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Lee Rosenthal

Subject: Question regarding Santa Monica Mountain Planning Commission

          Tony Alperin has asked whether the Santa Monica
Mountain Planning Commission is a state agency.  The
Commission is charged with developing a land use plan
for the Santa Monica Mountains which lie in Los Angeles
and Ventura Counties.  The Commission is created by
state statute, funded by the state and a state official
serves on the commission.  The remainder of the members
are either local elected officials or are appointed.

     If the Commission is a state agency, must the City
of Los Angeles disclose on its lobbyist employer reports,
the salary it pays the aides to a city councilman (who
serves on the Commission) for the staff work the aides
do in connection with the councilman's Commission duties?

     Also, can the city be deemed to have attempted to
influence the Commission if the Commission seeks to
convince the city to change its general plan so that it
conforms with the Commission plan for the Santa Monica
Mountains?

LCR:plh

sections.question:
Tony Alperin has asked whether the Santa Monica Mountain Planning Commission is a state agency. The Commission is charged with developing a land use plan for the Santa Monica Mountains which lie in Los Angeles and Ventura Counties. The Commission is created by state statute, funded by the state and a state official serves on the commission. The remainder of the members are either local elected officials or are appointed.

If the Commission is a state agency, must the City of Los Angeles disclose on its lobbyist employer reports, the salary it pays the aides to a city councilman (who serves on the Commission) for the staff work the aides do in connection with the councilman's Commission duties?

Also, can the city be deemed to have attempted to influence the Commission if the Commission seeks to convince the city to change its general plan so that it conforms with the Commission plan for the Santa Monica Mountains?
sections.conclusion: The Santa Monica Mountain Comprehensive Planning Commission is a state agency. The Commission (which has ceased to exist) was authorized by statute of the State of California. One member was a state officer, the Commission was financed completely by the State and the jurisdiction included Los Angeles and Ventura counties. While the Commission is a state agency, the city of Los Angeles is not required to disclose on its lobbyist employer report, the salaries paid to aides of a city councilman for the staff work the aides do in connection with the councilman's Commission duties. FPPC staff determined that the aides are assisting the city councilman in carrying out his official duties. Therefore, the city of Los Angeles would not be required to report the aide's salaries on the lobbyist employer report. (Government Code Section 86300(a)) Mr. Alperin has withdrawn the question raised with respect to the city attempting to influence the Commission.
sections.question_synthetic: Is the Santa Monica Mountain Comprehensive Planning Commission a state agency, and if so, must the City of Los Angeles disclose aide salaries for a city councilman's Commission duties on lobbyist employer reports? Can the city be deemed to have attempted to influence the Commission through its interactions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Santa Monica Mountain Commission is a state agency, but the City of Los Angeles need not disclose aide salaries on lobbyist employer reports since the aides are assisting the councilman in his official duties. The question regarding whether the city attempted to influence the Commission was withdrawn by the requester.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the internal memo from Lee Rosenthal and the FPPC's response to Tony Alperin. The question about city influence on the Commission was withdrawn by the requester, so only partial conclusions provided. Document structure is somewhat unconventional with the memo appearing after the response summary.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Tony Alperin has asked whether the Santa Monica Mountain Planning Commission is a state agency. The Commission is charged with developing a land use plan for the Santa Monica Mountains which lie in Los Angeles and Ventura Counties. The Commission is created by state statute, funded by the state and a state official serves on the commission. The remainder of the members are either local elected officials or are appointed.

If the Commission is a state agency, must the City of Los Angeles disclose on its lobbyist employer reports, the salary it pays the aides to a city councilman (who serves on the Commission) for the staff work the aides do in connection with the councilman's Commission duties?

Also, can the city be deemed to have attempted to influence the Commission if the Commission seeks to convince the city to change its general plan so that it conforms with the Commission plan for the Santa Monica Mountains?

CONCLUSION: The Santa Monica Mountain Comprehensive Planning Commission is a state agency. The Commission (which has ceased to exist) was authorized by statute of the State of California. One member was a state officer, the Commission was financed completely by the State and the jurisdiction included Los Angeles and Ventura counties. While the Commission is a state agency, the city of Los Angeles is not required to disclose on its lobbyist employer report, the salaries paid to aides of a city councilman for the staff work the aides do in connection with the councilman's Commission duties. FPPC staff determined that the aides are assisting the city councilman in carrying out his official duties. Therefore, the city of Los Angeles would not be required to report the aide's salaries on the lobbyist employer report. (Government Code Section 86300(a)) Mr. Alperin has withdrawn the question raised with respect to the city attempting to influence the Commission.

State of California Memorandum To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants Date : September 7, 197 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Lee Rosenthal Subject: Question regarding Santa Monica Mountain Planning Commission Tony Alperin has asked whether the Santa Monica Mountain Planning Commission is a state agency. The Commission is charged with developing a land use plan for the Santa Monica Mountains which lie in Los Angeles and Ventura Counties. The Commission is created by state statute, funded by the state and a state official serves on the commission. The remainder of the members are either local elected officials or are appointed. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the Santa Monica Mountain Comprehensive Planning Commission is a state agency, and that the City of Los Angeles is not required to disclose aide salaries related to a city councilman's Commission duties on lobbyist employer reports. The requester withdrew the question about whether the city attempted to influence the Commission.
